heading: 2 Pipes
text:
want run several functions turn, example, wanted find mean bill length penguin species group_by summarise (find functions Chapter 4).
nest one function inside .larger problems using function, solution becomes almost impossible read easy make mistakes forget brackets belong function.Another strategy make use intermediate objectsThis works better, can generate many intermediates can difficult ensure correct one used.popular alternative use pipes.
R code pipe |>
Pipes pass results one function directly next function.never need use pipes, can make code readable.
code:
summarise(group_by(penguins, species), mean = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))## # A tibble: 3 x 2
##   species    mean
## * <fct>     <dbl>
## 1 Adelie     38.8
## 2 Chinstrap  48.8
## 3 Gentoo     47.5
penguins_grouped <- group_by(penguins, species)
summarise(penguins_grouped, mean = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))## # A tibble: 3 x 2
##   species    mean
## * <fct>     <dbl>
## 1 Adelie     38.8
## 2 Chinstrap  48.8
## 3 Gentoo     47.5penguins |> 
  group_by(species) |>
  summarise(mean = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

heading: The old pipe %>%
text:
|> pipe introduced R version 4.1.
Previously, magrittr pipe %>% widely used, especially tidyverse functions.
see %>% lot code stackoverflow help sites.
cases old new pipes work exactly way.
Advantages |> pipe isslightly fasterdoesn’t need packages loading

heading: 2.1 A recipe for mashed potato
text:
pipe can read “”.
recipe mashed potato can read buy 1kg potatoes, peel , boil , .
code:
buy("potatoes", kg = "1") |> 
  peel() |> 
  boil(minutes = "15") |> 
  drain() |> 
  mash(add = list("salt", "milk", "butter")) |> 
  serve(decorate = "parsley")

heading: 2.2 The native R pipe |>
text:
pipe passes result code left pipe function right, puts first available argument.Soand rewritten asIf want put object passed pipe second argument, need name first, available.
want pipe penguins lm fit linear model, need penguins put data argument, second argument lm.
can force naming formula argument, data first available argument.complex arrangements, example piping object two separate commands can done writing function.
probably won’t often.can make pipe either typing directly, using RStudio keyboard shortcut Ctrl + Shift + m
(Mac, cmd + Shift + m).
may need set RStudio options.
Go Tools > Global Options > Code tick Use native pipe.
make code readable, put line break pipe.
code:
f <- "file.csv"
read_csv(file = f)f |>
 read_csv()# un-named argument = fails
penguins |>
  lm(bill_length ~ species)

# named first argument, penguins pipes into second argument
penguins |>
  lm(formula = bill_length ~ species)

heading: Contributors
text: Richard J. Telford

heading: 3.1 Tidy data
text:
can start analysing plotting data, often need tidy .
Tidy data standardised way structure dataset makes much easier process, analyse plot data.
Functions tidyr dplyr packages, part tidyverse, can useful tidying data.tidy dataEvery column variable.Every row observation.Every cell single value.process tidying data convert data data meets standard.

heading: 3.2 separate() multiple values
text:
cell contains multiple values, can use tidyr::separate().example, small dataset, site code plot number combined one column separated hyphen.can use separate() split site plot separate columns.
default, separate() splits data non-alphanumeric values.
behaviour can controlled sep argument.
code:
dat <- tribble(~id, ~value,
       "A-1", 1,
       "A-2", 2,
       "B-1", 3)

dat## # A tibble: 3 x 2
##   id    value
##   <chr> <dbl>
## 1 A-1       1
## 2 A-2       2
## 3 B-1       3
dat %>% separate(col = id, into = c("site", "plot"))## # A tibble: 3 x 3
##   site  plot  value
##   <chr> <chr> <dbl>
## 1 A     1         1
## 2 A     2         2
## 3 B     1         3

heading: 3.3 Reshaping data - wide to long
text:
common need reshape data make tidy.
can done pivot_* functions.
Figure 3.1: Wide data long data pivot_longer() pivot_wider() .
Bergen climate data Wikipedia (NB demonstration wikipedia good source climate data - use seklima Norwegian data).might nice way present data tidy data:
row observation; column variable.can use pivot_longer() reshape data.
months, selected cols argument column names (see section 4.2.1 syntax used ) become new variable name set names_to, data values get put column named values_to argument.data now tidier, probably useful reshape data , column climate variable.
can pivot_wider().
code:
## # A tibble: 4 x 13
##   Måned    Jan   Feb   Mar   Apr   Mai   Jun   Jul   Aug   Sep   Okt   Nov   Des
##   <chr>  <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Norma…   3.6   4     5.9   9.1  14    16.8  17.6  17.4  14.2  11.2   6.9   4.7
## 2 Døgnm…   1.7   1.7   3.3   5.8  10.4  13.1  14.2  14.2  11.5   8.8   4.8   2.7
## 3 Norma…  -0.4  -0.5   0.9   3     7.2  10.2  11.5  11.6   9.1   6.6   2.8   0.6
## 4 Nedbø… 190   152   170   114   106   132   148   190   283   271   259   235
bergen_klima_long <- bergen_klima %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = Jan:Des, names_to = "Month", values_to = "value")
bergen_klima_long## # A tibble: 48 x 3
##    Måned                 Month value
##    <chr>                 <chr> <dbl>
##  1 Normal maks. temp. °C Jan     3.6
##  2 Normal maks. temp. °C Feb     4  
##  3 Normal maks. temp. °C Mar     5.9
##  4 Normal maks. temp. °C Apr     9.1
##  5 Normal maks. temp. °C Mai    14  
##  6 Normal maks. temp. °C Jun    16.8
##  7 Normal maks. temp. °C Jul    17.6
##  8 Normal maks. temp. °C Aug    17.4
##  9 Normal maks. temp. °C Sep    14.2
## 10 Normal maks. temp. °C Okt    11.2
## # … with 38 more rows

heading: 3.4 Reshaping data - long to wide
text: can tell pivot_wider() column contains become column names data names_from values_from, respectively.data now convenient format ploting analysis.
code:
bergen_klima_wider <- bergen_klima_long %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = "Måned", values_from = "value")

bergen_klima_wider## # A tibble: 12 x 5
##    Month `Normal maks. temp… `Døgnmiddeltemp. … `Normal min. temp… `Nedbør (mm)`
##    <chr>               <dbl>              <dbl>              <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1 Jan                   3.6                1.7               -0.4           190
##  2 Feb                   4                  1.7               -0.5           152
##  3 Mar                   5.9                3.3                0.9           170
##  4 Apr                   9.1                5.8                3             114
##  5 Mai                  14                 10.4                7.2           106
##  6 Jun                  16.8               13.1               10.2           132
##  7 Jul                  17.6               14.2               11.5           148
##  8 Aug                  17.4               14.2               11.6           190
##  9 Sep                  14.2               11.5                9.1           283
## 10 Okt                  11.2                8.8                6.6           271
## 11 Nov                   6.9                4.8                2.8           259
## 12 Des                   4.7                2.7                0.6           235

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
text:
Data frames usually convenient objects storing, plotting analysing data R.
also need able manipulate data data frames.tutorial show manipulate data frames using dplyr package, part tidyverse.Everything can done dplyr can done base R.
dplyr solution usually easier write understand, can faster.many function dplyr, tutorial focuses functions find useful.

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.0.1 Load the package
text: can load dplyr withBut usually convenient load tidyverse get ggplot2, dplyr, readr useful packages one command.
code:
library("dplyr")
library("tidyverse")

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.0.2 More feedback tidylog
text: can get feedback dplyr functions done data loading tidylog package., tidylog helped identified bugs code.
code:
library("tidylog")
# if you are using conflicted, you will need to set conflict_prefer() for all dplyr function
# use purrr::map to iterate over tidylog functions 
# library(conflicted)
# map(getNamespaceExports("tidylog"), ~conflict_prefer(.x, winner = "tidylog"))

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.1 The penguins dataset
text:
tutorial use penguins dataset palmerpenguins package.
dataset includes measurements three species penguin.
code:
# Load the data
data("penguins", package = "palmerpenguins")
# Show the data
penguins## # A tibble: 344 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## # … with 341 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.2 Selecting columns with select()
text:
can choose columns data frame want select().first argument data, supplied pipe |>, next arguments names columns want.
names need quote marks.equivalent base R codeRemember want use output code analysis, need assign object name <-.
code:
#select species, bill_length_mm & bill_depth_mm
penguins %>% 
  select(species, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)## select: dropped 5 variables (island, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, sex, year)## # A tibble: 344 x 3
##   species bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##   <fct>            <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1 Adelie            39.1          18.7
## 2 Adelie            39.5          17.4
## 3 Adelie            40.3          18  
## # … with 341 more rows
#select species, bill_length_mm & bill_depth_mm
penguins[, c("species", "bill_length_mm", "bill_depth_mm")] ## # A tibble: 344 x 3
##   species bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##   <fct>            <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1 Adelie            39.1          18.7
## 2 Adelie            39.5          17.4
## 3 Adelie            40.3          18  
## # … with 341 more rows

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.2.1 select() helpers
text:
Sometimes don’t want write names columns want select.
might even know advance.
Fortunately helper functions.want select() adjacent columns, can use notation first:last.Sometimes easier remove columns don’t want.
can putting - front column name.pattern column names want select (remove), helper functions.
example, select columns start “bill”, can use starts_with().Conversely, want select columns end “mm”, can use ends_with().
contains() flexible matches() powerful helper functions, using regular expressions identify columns (see regular expression tutorial).Sometimes, might want select columns certain type.
example, select numeric columns can use .numeric function inside select() helper ()..* functions exist, example, .character text, lubridate::.Date dates.can also select columns number (1 first column), generally bad idea makes code difficult understand new column added, column order changed, code break.strategies works best context dependent.
code:
#select species to bill_depth_mm
penguins %>% select(species:bill_depth_mm)## select: dropped 4 variables (flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, sex, year)## # A tibble: 344 x 4
##   species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##   <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7
## 2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4
## 3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18  
## # … with 341 more rows
#select everything but year and sex
penguins %>% select(-year, -sex)## select: dropped 2 variables (sex, year)## # A tibble: 344 x 6
##   species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
##   <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
## 1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
## 2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
## 3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
## # … with 341 more rows
#select bill_length_mm & bill_depth_mm" 
penguins %>% select(starts_with("bill"))## select: dropped 6 variables (species, island, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, sex, …)## # A tibble: 344 x 2
##   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##            <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1           39.1          18.7
## 2           39.5          17.4
## 3           40.3          18  
## # … with 341 more rows
penguins %>% select(where(is.numeric)) # No brackets on the function## select: dropped 3 variables (species, island, sex)## # A tibble: 344 x 5
##   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g  year
##            <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int> <int>
## 1           39.1          18.7               181        3750  2007
## 2           39.5          17.4               186        3800  2007
## 3           40.3          18                 195        3250  2007
## # … with 341 more rows

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.3 Renaming columns with rename
text:
can use rename() rename columnsThe syntax new_name = current_name.can also rename column selecting.
convenient using select() anyway.
code:
penguins %>% rename(Species = species)## rename: renamed one variable (Species)## # A tibble: 344 x 8
##   Species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## # … with 341 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>
penguins %>% select(Species = species)## select: renamed one variable (Species) and dropped 7 variables## # A tibble: 344 x 1
##   Species
##   <fct>  
## 1 Adelie 
## 2 Adelie 
## 3 Adelie 
## # … with 341 more rows

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.4 Moving columns with relocate()
text:
Sometimes useful reorder columns.
never necessary data analysis plotting, can needed making table presentation.default move named column first, ..arguments let move column position.
code:
penguins %>% relocate(island)## relocate: columns reordered (island, species, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, …)## # A tibble: 344 x 8
##   island species bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>  <fct>            <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Torge… Adelie            39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Torge… Adelie            39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Torge… Adelie            40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## # … with 341 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.5 Filtering rows with filter()
text: Filtering rows meet condition common task.example, filter rows penguins bill length greater 40 mm, can useThis filter row condition TRUE.base R equivalent isGenerally, filter makes easier understand, especially complex criteria.tests include== exactly equals. Often bad idea use numeric datanear safe function testing equality numeric data tolerance rounding errors.!= equal < less <= less equal > greater >= greater equal tois.na() filtering missing values.() filtering values range%% used want test value vector
code:
penguins %>% filter(bill_length_mm > 40)## filter: removed 102 rows (30%), 242 rows remaining## # A tibble: 242 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           42            20.2              190        4250 <NA> 
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           41.1          17.6              182        3200 fema…
## # … with 239 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>
penguins[penguins$bill_length_mm > 40, ]## # A tibble: 244 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## 2 <NA>    <NA>             NA            NA                 NA          NA <NA> 
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           42            20.2              190        4250 <NA> 
## # … with 241 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>
sqrt(2) ^ 2 == 2 # should be true, but rounding errors ## [1] FALSE
sqrt(2) ^ 2 - 2 # the difference## [1] 4.440892e-16
near(sqrt(2) ^ 2, 2) # safe alternative## [1] TRUE
penguins %>% 
  filter(species %in% c("Adelie", "Chinstrap"))## filter: removed 124 rows (36%), 220 rows remaining## # A tibble: 220 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## # … with 217 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>
#equivalent to 
penguins %>% 
  filter(species == "Adelie" | species == "Chinstrap") # with many alternatives, this gets long## filter: removed 124 rows (36%), 220 rows remaining## # A tibble: 220 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## # … with 217 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.5.1 Filtering on multiple criteria
text:
want filter multiple criteria, need decide whether want criteria TRUE (Boolean logic), one TRUE (Boolean logic).want criteria TRUE, can separate comma (& want explicit).want rows criteria TRUE, can separate |.can negate criterion putting ! front .
filter rows bills longer 40 mm can useOf course, example, also use <= test.
code:
penguins %>% 
  filter(bill_length_mm > 40, bill_depth_mm > 18)## filter: removed 263 rows (76%), 81 rows remaining## # A tibble: 81 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           42            20.2              190        4250 <NA> 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           42.5          20.7              197        4500 male 
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           46            21.5              194        4200 male 
## # … with 78 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>
penguins %>% 
  filter(bill_length_mm > 40 | bill_depth_mm > 18)## filter: removed 53 rows (15%), 291 rows remaining## # A tibble: 291 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           36.7          19.3              193        3450 fema…
## # … with 288 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>
penguins %>% filter(!bill_length_mm > 40)## filter: removed 244 rows (71%), 100 rows remaining## # A tibble: 100 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           36.7          19.3              193        3450 fema…
## # … with 97 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.5.2 Common errors
text:
commonest error use single = rather ==.
latter test equality.
, error message quite helpful.Another common error forget quote strings.
code:
penguins %>% filter(species = "Chinstrap")## Error: Problem with `filter()` input `..1`.
## x Input `..1` is named.
## ℹ This usually means that you've used `=` instead of `==`.
## ℹ Did you mean `species == "Chinstrap"`?
penguins %>% filter(species == Chinstrap)## Error: Problem with `filter()` input `..1`.
## x object 'Chinstrap' not found
## ℹ Input `..1` is `species == Chinstrap`.

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.6 Slicing the data with slice()
text:
Sometimes useful extract rows row number.can use negative numbers remove rows.
careful using slice() data change, different rows may returned.
code:
penguins %>% slice(3:7)## slice: removed 339 rows (99%), 5 rows remaining## # A tibble: 5 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           NA            NA                 NA          NA <NA> 
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           36.7          19.3              193        3450 fema…
## 4 Adelie  Torge…           39.3          20.6              190        3650 male 
## 5 Adelie  Torge…           38.9          17.8              181        3625 fema…
## # … with 1 more variable: year <int>

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.7 Distinct rows with distinct()
text:
duplicates data, can remove distinct().
distinct() extra arguments remove duplicate rows.interested columns, can supply names columns.columns removed unless argument .keep_all = TRUE used.
code:
penguins %>% distinct()## distinct: no rows removed## # A tibble: 344 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## # … with 341 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>
penguins %>% distinct(island)## distinct: removed 341 rows (99%), 3 rows remaining## # A tibble: 3 x 1
##   island   
##   <fct>    
## 1 Torgersen
## 2 Biscoe   
## 3 Dream

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.8 Random rows
text:
Sometimes want sample rows random data.frame.
can done slice_sample().
can either sample constant n rows constant fraction rows depending whether n prop argument used.
code:
penguins %>% slice_sample(n = 10)## slice_sample: removed 334 rows (97%), 10 rows remaining## # A tibble: 10 x 8
##    species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int>
##  1 Gentoo  Biscoe           43.3          14                208        4575
##  2 Chinst… Dream            43.5          18.1              202        3400
##  3 Adelie  Biscoe           37.6          19.1              194        3750
##  4 Adelie  Dream            37.8          18.1              193        3750
##  5 Gentoo  Biscoe           43.5          15.2              213        4650
##  6 Gentoo  Biscoe           46.1          15.1              215        5100
##  7 Gentoo  Biscoe           50.7          15                223        5550
##  8 Chinst… Dream            45.2          17.8              198        3950
##  9 Chinst… Dream            42.4          17.3              181        3600
## 10 Adelie  Torge…           38.8          17.6              191        3275
## # … with 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.9 Mutating and adding columns with mutate
text:
function mutate() can add new column replace existing one.make new column called body_mass_kg can useThere lots functions useful use mutate.
function returns either single value many values data can used.mutate() useful cleaning data.See text manipulation tutorial cleaning text stringr package.See date time tutorial cleaning dates times lubridate package.
code:
penguins %>% 
  mutate(body_mass_kg = body_mass_g / 1000)## mutate: new variable 'body_mass_kg' (double) with 95 unique values and 1% NA## # A tibble: 344 x 9
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250 fema…
## # … with 341 more rows, and 2 more variables: year <int>, body_mass_kg <dbl>

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.10 Summarising data with summarise()
text:
summarise() lets us summarise data.
can use want calculate summary statistic data.
Remember separate arguments comma.columns created summarise() grouping columns (see ) kept.
code:
penguins %>% summarise(
  flipper_len_mean = mean(flipper_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE), 
  flipper_len_sd = sd(flipper_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE)
  )## summarise: now one row and 2 columns, ungrouped## # A tibble: 1 x 2
##   flipper_len_mean flipper_len_sd
##              <dbl>          <dbl>
## 1             201.           14.1

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.10.1 Summarising multiple columns
text:
Sometimes want summarise multiple columns time.
can done across() helper function.
across() needs told columns process function functions use.can also use across() mutate() mutate several columns time.
code:
penguins %>%
   summarise(across(c(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm), .fns = mean, na.rm = TRUE))## summarise: now one row and 2 columns, ungrouped## # A tibble: 1 x 2
##   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##            <dbl>         <dbl>
## 1           43.9          17.2
#using a list of functions
penguins %>%
  summarise(across(.cols = starts_with("bill"), .fns = list(sd = sd, mean = mean), na.rm = TRUE))## summarise: now one row and 4 columns, ungrouped## # A tibble: 1 x 4
##   bill_length_mm_sd bill_length_mm_mean bill_depth_mm_sd bill_depth_mm_mean
##               <dbl>               <dbl>            <dbl>              <dbl>
## 1              5.46                43.9             1.97               17.2

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.11 Grouping data with group_by
text:
group_by() changes way many dplyr functions work.
Instead working entire dataset, now work group dataTo find mean flipper length species, need group_by() species summarise().Grouped data can ungrouped ungroup().
can help prevent surprises!
code:
penguins %>% 
  group_by(species) %>% 
  summarise(mean_flipper_length = mean(flipper_length_mm))## group_by: one grouping variable (species)## summarise: now 3 rows and 2 columns, ungrouped## # A tibble: 3 x 2
##   species   mean_flipper_length
## * <fct>                   <dbl>
## 1 Adelie                    NA 
## 2 Chinstrap                196.
## 3 Gentoo                    NA

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.12 Sorting with arrange()
text:
sort data frame one variables can use arrange().sort smallest first.
reverse sort order, use desc()
code:
penguins %>% arrange(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)## # A tibble: 344 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Dream            32.1          15.5              188        3050 fema…
## 2 Adelie  Dream            33.1          16.1              178        2900 fema…
## 3 Adelie  Torge…           33.5          19                190        3600 fema…
## # … with 341 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>
penguins %>% arrange(desc(bill_length_mm), desc(bill_depth_mm))## # A tibble: 344 x 8
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Gentoo  Biscoe           59.6          17                230        6050 male 
## 2 Chinst… Dream            58            17.8              181        3700 fema…
## 3 Gentoo  Biscoe           55.9          17                228        5600 male 
## # … with 341 more rows, and 1 more variable: year <int>

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.13 Counting rows with count() and n()
text:
function n can count many rows group (entire data frame grouped).
can used either mutate() summarise().count()count() concise, group_by() summarise() useful need calculate variables (example mean standard deviation).
code:
penguins %>% 
  group_by(species) %>% 
  summarise(n = n())## group_by: one grouping variable (species)## summarise: now 3 rows and 2 columns, ungrouped## # A tibble: 3 x 2
##   species       n
## * <fct>     <int>
## 1 Adelie      152
## 2 Chinstrap    68
## 3 Gentoo      124
penguins %>% 
  count(species)## count: now 3 rows and 2 columns, ungrouped## # A tibble: 3 x 2
##   species       n
## * <fct>     <int>
## 1 Adelie      152
## 2 Chinstrap    68
## 3 Gentoo      124

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.14 Common problems

chapter: 4 Working with single tables in dplyr
heading: 4.14.1 Non standard names
text:
Ideally column names follow standard rules naming objects R - UPPER lower case letters, numbers, “.” "_" first character letter (dot want invisible object).
Sometimes import data, non-standard names spaces extra characters.
need refer column name doesn’t follow rules, need enclose back-ticks.sometimes best rename columns make easier refer .
janitor::clean_names() efficient making easy--use names.
code:
df <- tibble(`Region/Country` = "Norway", value = 42)
df## # A tibble: 1 x 2
##   `Region/Country` value
##   <chr>            <dbl>
## 1 Norway              42
df %>% rename(region_country = `Region/Country`)## rename: renamed one variable (region_country)## # A tibble: 1 x 2
##   region_country value
##   <chr>          <dbl>
## 1 Norway            42

chapter: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr
heading: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr
text:
dplyr functions looked far work single data frame.
chapter introduces joins, work pair data frames least one identifying variable common, bind_* functions bind two data frames together.

chapter: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr
heading: 5.1 Merging data frames with mutating joins
text:
Mutating joins combine two data frames matching rows according one identifying variables data frames.commonly used mutating join left join.
Left joins take rows first data set, rows second data frame values identifying variable match first (Fig. 5.1).
Figure 5.1: Left join. coloured column contains identifiers.
duplicate values identifying column, (Fig. 5.2) causes matching rows duplicated.
number rows result increases expecting , can indicate identifier unique.
Figure 5.2: Left join. coloured column contains identifiers.
Left joins implemented dplyr left_join().Let use left_join() add location island penguin data set.first two arguments left_join() data frames, third argument tells join column make join .
, joining single column name data frames.
possible join multiple columns columns different names dataset.three four islands penguin_islands data penguins data set.
Different variants mutating joins treat different ways.left_join() takes rows first (left) data frame matching rows second (right).right_join() opposite left_join(), taking rows second (right) data frame matching rows first.inner_join() take rows match data frames.full_join() take rows data frames.cases, missing values given NA.
code:
# location of the three islands in the penguins dataset.

penguin_islands <- tribble(
  ~ island, ~ Latitude, ~ Longitude, # tribble is a convenient way to make small datasets
  "Torgersen", -64.766667,-64.083333,
  "Biscoe", -64.818569, -63.775636,
  "Dream",  -64.733333, -64.233333, 
  "Alpha", -64.316667, -63)

penguin_islands## # A tibble: 4 x 3
##   island    Latitude Longitude
##   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 Torgersen    -64.8     -64.1
## 2 Biscoe       -64.8     -63.8
## 3 Dream        -64.7     -64.2
## 4 Alpha        -64.3     -63
penguin_small <- penguins %>% 
  group_by(species) %>% 
  slice(1:2) # small version of data for easy viewing


left_join(penguin_small, penguin_islands, by = "island")## # A tibble: 6 x 10
## # Groups:   species [3]
##   species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g sex  
##   <fct>   <chr>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int> <fct>
## 1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750 male 
## 2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800 fema…
## 3 Chinst… Dream            46.5          17.9              192        3500 fema…
## 4 Chinst… Dream            50            19.5              196        3900 male 
## 5 Gentoo  Biscoe           46.1          13.2              211        4500 fema…
## 6 Gentoo  Biscoe           50            16.3              230        5700 male 
## # … with 3 more variables: year <int>, Latitude <dbl>, Longitude <dbl>

chapter: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr
heading: 5.2 All possible combinations
text:
mutating joins described give rows data frame identifying variables match.
Sometimes want possible combinations rows.
known Cartesian product can generated crosing().
crosing() works data frames well vectors shown .
code:
crossing(a = letters[1:3], b = 1:2)## # A tibble: 6 x 2
##   a         b
##   <chr> <int>
## 1 a         1
## 2 a         2
## 3 b         1
## 4 b         2
## 5 c         1
## 6 c         2

chapter: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr
heading: 5.3 Filtering joins
text:
Filtering joins let filter one dataset according whether rows match second dataset.semi_join() finds rows matching row
Figure 5.3: Left join. coloured column contains identifiers.
anti_join() finds rows matching row
Figure 5.4: Left join. coloured column contains identifiers.
can useful cleaning data find problems.
code:
penguin_islands %>% 
  semi_join(penguins, by = "island")## # A tibble: 3 x 3
##   island    Latitude Longitude
##   <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 Torgersen    -64.8     -64.1
## 2 Biscoe       -64.8     -63.8
## 3 Dream        -64.7     -64.2
penguin_islands %>% 
  anti_join(penguins, by = "island")## # A tibble: 1 x 3
##   island Latitude Longitude
##   <chr>     <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 Alpha     -64.3       -63

chapter: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr
heading: 5.4 Binding data frames together
text: two data frames want combine can one bind_* functions.

chapter: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr
heading: 5.4.1 More columns - bind_cols()
text:
data frames contain information observations, can combined bind_cols().data1, data2, data3 can combined make one data frame many columnsbind_cols() expects row order datasets, check .
checks number rows data frame.
possible, use join instead.
code: bind_cols(data1, data2, data3)

chapter: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr
heading: 5.4.2 More rows - bind_rows()
text:
data frame contain observations (rows), typically least columns, can combined bind_rows().
useful , example, data two years need combining.One feature bind_rows() find useful .id argument makes extra column identifier.
code:
svalbard_islands <-  tribble( ~ island, ~ Latitude, ~ Longitude,
   "Nordaustlandet", 79.558405, 24.017351,
 "Prins Karls Forland", 78.554090, 11.256545)

bind_rows(
  Palmer = penguin_islands, 
  Svalbard = svalbard_islands, 
  .id = "Archipelago")## # A tibble: 6 x 4
##   Archipelago island              Latitude Longitude
##   <chr>       <chr>                  <dbl>     <dbl>
## 1 Palmer      Torgersen              -64.8     -64.1
## 2 Palmer      Biscoe                 -64.8     -63.8
## 3 Palmer      Dream                  -64.7     -64.2
## 4 Palmer      Alpha                  -64.3     -63  
## 5 Svalbard    Nordaustlandet          79.6      24.0
## 6 Svalbard    Prins Karls Forland     78.6      11.3

chapter: 5 Working with multiple tables in dplyr

heading: 6 Handling missing values
text:
Many datasets missing values.
represent data measured, perhaps instrument failure, impossible measure, example water temperature 200m sea 100m deep.Missing values R represented NA.tutorial shows cope missing values R, focusing manipulating data tidyverse package, running statistical analyses, making figures ggplot2.penguins dataset palmerpenguins used example.

heading: 6.1.1 Detecting missing values
text: NA values can detected function .na (NB lower case).use , example, find number missing values column penguins data.
code:
x <- c(0, 7, NA)
is.na(x)## [1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE
penguins %>% 
  summarise(n_missing = sum(is.na(bill_length_mm)))## # A tibble: 1 x 1
##   n_missing
##       <int>
## 1         2

heading: 6.1.2 Importing data with missing values
text:
import text file (e.g., csv file) blank cells, cells “NA” treated NA.
coded missing values something else, can use na argument read_delimIn readxl::read_excel, default na argument just blank cells made NA, values can added way read_delim.
code:
# set blank cells, "NA" or "missing" to NA  
read_delim(file = "my_file.csv", na = c("", "NA", "missing"))

heading: 6.1.3 Removing rows with missing values
text:
can remove rows NA particular columns data frame using drop_na.
example, remove rows penguins data set NA bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm columns, useAlternatively, can use filter .na.
useful removing NA one several arguments filter.might see code using na.omit remove rows missing values data frame.
can dangerous remove rows NA column, just columns interested .
code:
penguins %>% 
  drop_na(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm)## # A tibble: 342 x 8
##    species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750
##  2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800
##  3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250
##  4 Adelie  Torge…           36.7          19.3              193        3450
##  5 Adelie  Torge…           39.3          20.6              190        3650
##  6 Adelie  Torge…           38.9          17.8              181        3625
##  7 Adelie  Torge…           39.2          19.6              195        4675
##  8 Adelie  Torge…           34.1          18.1              193        3475
##  9 Adelie  Torge…           42            20.2              190        4250
## 10 Adelie  Torge…           37.8          17.1              186        3300
## # … with 332 more rows, and 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>
penguins %>% 
  filter(species == "Gentoo", !is.na(bill_length_mm))## # A tibble: 123 x 8
##    species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int>
##  1 Gentoo  Biscoe           46.1          13.2              211        4500
##  2 Gentoo  Biscoe           50            16.3              230        5700
##  3 Gentoo  Biscoe           48.7          14.1              210        4450
##  4 Gentoo  Biscoe           50            15.2              218        5700
##  5 Gentoo  Biscoe           47.6          14.5              215        5400
##  6 Gentoo  Biscoe           46.5          13.5              210        4550
##  7 Gentoo  Biscoe           45.4          14.6              211        4800
##  8 Gentoo  Biscoe           46.7          15.3              219        5200
##  9 Gentoo  Biscoe           43.3          13.4              209        4400
## 10 Gentoo  Biscoe           46.8          15.4              215        5150
## # … with 113 more rows, and 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

heading: 6.1.4 Replacing missing values
text:
Sometimes want replace NA another value.
Perhaps want make plot NA values labelled unknown, know NA values actually zeros, NA values detection limit instrument want replace half detection limit.can use tidyr::replace_na ., code replaces missing values bill length mean value bill length.
code:
penguins %>% 
  mutate(
    bill_length_mm = replace_na(
      data = bill_length_mm, 
      replace = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))
  )## # A tibble: 344 x 8
##    species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750
##  2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800
##  3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250
##  4 Adelie  Torge…           43.9          NA                 NA          NA
##  5 Adelie  Torge…           36.7          19.3              193        3450
##  6 Adelie  Torge…           39.3          20.6              190        3650
##  7 Adelie  Torge…           38.9          17.8              181        3625
##  8 Adelie  Torge…           39.2          19.6              195        4675
##  9 Adelie  Torge…           34.1          18.1              193        3475
## 10 Adelie  Torge…           42            20.2              190        4250
## # … with 334 more rows, and 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

heading: 6.1.5 Replacing NA in factors
text: code previous section won’t work try replace NA factor.warning indicates, problem “missing” one levels factor sex.Instead need use function forcats::fct_explicit_na (forcats package part tidyverse manipulating factors).
code:
penguins %>% 
  mutate(sex = replace_na(sex, "missing")) %>% 
  distinct(sex)## Warning in `[<-.factor`(`*tmp*`, !is_complete(data), value = "missing"): invalid
## factor level, NA generated## # A tibble: 3 x 1
##   sex   
##   <fct> 
## 1 male  
## 2 female
## 3 <NA>
penguins %>% 
  mutate(sex = fct_explicit_na(sex, na_level = "missing")) %>% 
  count(sex)## # A tibble: 3 x 2
##   sex         n
## * <fct>   <int>
## 1 female    165
## 2 male      168
## 3 missing    11

heading: 6.1.6 Replacing NA with values from another vector
text:
Sometimes vector NA want replace missing values values second vector.
coalesce can ., first value x NA, first value y used.
second value x NA, can used.
.

heading: 6.1.7 Setting mising values
text:
data sets use number represent missing value, example -9999.
Obviously, calculations data set containing -9999 missing value, results seriously wrong.
Instead need replace values NA.
can dplyr::na_if.

heading: 6.1.8 Missing values and dplyr filter
text:
dplyr::filter returns rows condition strictly TRUE.
usually want: want filter penguins long bills penguins dataset don’t want birds unknown bill length.Occasionally want keep rows NA.
Perhaps column comments, many NA, want remove rows non-NA values meet criterion.| means Boolean logic.
code:
penguins %>% 
  filter(sex == "Female" | is.na(sex))## # A tibble: 11 x 8
##    species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torge…           NA            NA                 NA          NA
##  2 Adelie  Torge…           34.1          18.1              193        3475
##  3 Adelie  Torge…           42            20.2              190        4250
##  4 Adelie  Torge…           37.8          17.1              186        3300
##  5 Adelie  Torge…           37.8          17.3              180        3700
##  6 Adelie  Dream            37.5          18.9              179        2975
##  7 Gentoo  Biscoe           44.5          14.3              216        4100
##  8 Gentoo  Biscoe           46.2          14.4              214        4650
##  9 Gentoo  Biscoe           47.3          13.8              216        4725
## 10 Gentoo  Biscoe           44.5          15.7              217        4875
## 11 Gentoo  Biscoe           NA            NA                 NA          NA
## # … with 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

heading: 6.1.9 Missing values and dplyr if_else and case_when
text:
Sometimes useful set values NA if_else (choice two outcomes) case_when (choice two outcomes).possible values returned if_else case_when need type (character, numeric, integer, logical).
types inconsistent, error produced.
useful forcing consistency helps avoid unexpected behaviour.problem occurs NA treated logical vector, values characters.
solution use NA_character_ NA correct type.typed NA include NA_real_ numeric values NA_integer integer values.
code:
x <- 0:2
case_when(
  x == 0 ~ NA,
  x == 1 ~ "One",
  x == 2 ~ "Two"
)## Error: must be a logical vector, not a character vector.
x <- 0:2
case_when(
  x == 0 ~ NA_character_,
  x == 1 ~ "One",
  x == 2 ~ "Two"
)## [1] NA    "One" "Two"

heading: 6.2.1 NA arithemetic
text:
five plus unknown number?
answer , course, unknown.NA contagious calculations: one value NA result NA.
effects many descriptive statistics.solution use na.rm argument functions exclude NA calculation.
code:
5 + NA## [1] NA
x <- c(1, 7, NA)
sum(x)## [1] NA
mean(x)## [1] NA
min(x)## [1] NA
sum(x, na.rm = TRUE)## [1] 8
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)## [1] 4
min(x, na.rm = TRUE)## [1] 1

heading: 6.2.2 NA in correlations and covariances
text:
functions calculating correlation, cor, covariance, cov, work little differently functions can work two vectors matrix data frame.
use argument used control NA treated.default, values NA either vector, result also NA.
want find correlation two vectors without NA, useIf matrix (data frame), want calculate correlation matrix, use = "complete.obs" calculate using just rows NA, use = "pairwise.complete.obs" calculate correlation pair variables using complete pairs observations variables.
code:
cor(x = penguins$bill_length_mm,
    y = penguins$bill_depth_mm, 
    use = "pairwise.complete.obs")## [1] -0.2350529
penguins %>% 
  select(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g) %>% 
  cor(use = "pairwise.complete.obs") ##                   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
## bill_length_mm         1.0000000    -0.2350529         0.6561813   0.5951098
## bill_depth_mm         -0.2350529     1.0000000        -0.5838512  -0.4719156
## flipper_length_mm      0.6561813    -0.5838512         1.0000000   0.8712018
## body_mass_g            0.5951098    -0.4719156         0.8712018   1.0000000

heading: 6.2.3 Missing values and regession models
text:
default regression models lm, glm, lmer remove case NA either response predictors.
behaviour controlled na.action argument.
great interested model coefficients, can cause problems NA data want add residuals, fitted values, predictions original data frame plotting.problem fewer observation model (omitted NA) original data frame (still ).can make work using na.action = na.exclude pad fitted values NA length original data.na.exclude NA still excluded model fitting - models allow NA values.comparing models, example anova, models need fit dataset.
can cause problems NA caused different numbers observations removed.easiest solution remove rows NA predictor fitting models.
code:
mod <- lm(bill_length_mm ~ body_mass_g, data = penguins)

penguins %>% mutate(fit = fitted(mod))## Error: Problem with `mutate()` input `fit`.
## x Input `fit` can't be recycled to size 344.
## ℹ Input `fit` is `fitted(mod)`.
## ℹ Input `fit` must be size 344 or 1, not 342.
mod <- lm(bill_length_mm ~ body_mass_g, data = penguins, na.action = na.exclude)

penguins %>% mutate(fit = fitted(mod))## # A tibble: 344 x 9
##    species island bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_… body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>            <int>       <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torge…           39.1          18.7              181        3750
##  2 Adelie  Torge…           39.5          17.4              186        3800
##  3 Adelie  Torge…           40.3          18                195        3250
##  4 Adelie  Torge…           NA            NA                 NA          NA
##  5 Adelie  Torge…           36.7          19.3              193        3450
##  6 Adelie  Torge…           39.3          20.6              190        3650
##  7 Adelie  Torge…           38.9          17.8              181        3625
##  8 Adelie  Torge…           39.2          19.6              195        4675
##  9 Adelie  Torge…           34.1          18.1              193        3475
## 10 Adelie  Torge…           42            20.2              190        4250
## # … with 334 more rows, and 3 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>, fit <dbl>
mod2 <- lm(bill_length_mm ~ body_mass_g + sex, data = penguins, na.action = na.exclude)

anova(mod, mod2)## Error in anova.lmlist(object, ...): models were not all fitted to the same size of dataset

heading: 6.2.4 Imputing missing values
text:
shown , observations missing values omitted model.
predictor many missing values, may better exclude predictor model avoid losing many observations.alternative impute missing data.
done caution can bias results, especially substantial proportion data imputed.
hand, missing data randomly distributed, omitting observations missing data can also bias results.several ways can used impute missing values.simplest replace missing value mean median variable shown section 6.1.4.complex methods use multivariate relationship predictors estimate missing values.
Several R packages can help , e.g., mice.
Yadav Roychoudhury (2018) compare performance popular methods.

heading: 6.3 Missing values and ggplot2
text:
default, missing values x y aesthetics dropped ggplot warning, whereas missing values colour fill aesthetics shown grey.
behaviour can controlled na.value argument relevant scale_*_* function.
code:
#x or y NA
p <- tibble(
  x = 1:5, 
  y = c(1, 2, NA, 4, 5),
  colour = c(1, 2, 3, 4, NA)
) %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = x, y = y, colour = colour)) +
  geom_point(size = 3)

p## Warning: Removed 1 rows containing missing values (geom_point).
# change defaults
p + 
  scale_y_continuous(na.value = 0) +
  scale_colour_continuous(na.value = "hotpink")

heading: 6.4 Related concepts
text: NaN Inf related NA.

heading: 6.4.1 NaN
text:
NaN represents Number.
NaN can generated, example, taking log negative number.
can tested .nan()
code:
x <- log(c(1, -1, NA))
x## [1]   0 NaN  NA
is.nan(x)## [1] FALSE  TRUE FALSE

heading: 6.4.2 Inf
text:
Inf -Inf represent positive negative infinite numbers respectively.
can generated, example, dividing zero, tested .infinite()
code:
x <- c(-Inf, 0, Inf)
is.infinite(x)## [1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE

heading: 6.4.3 Comparison of tests
text: test .finite() TRUE value numeric NA, NaN, Inf -Inf.
